narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have the door close like this isn't easy, and I'm here to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam the way it did, almost like piecing together a story from the clues in your background. At the heart of it, your Year 1 grades placed you in the 2nd decile at school, which stood out as the biggest signal of struggle ahead, painting a picture of early challenges that often snowball in law studies. Building on that, your Year 3 decile of 1st—the very bottom—reinforced this strongly, suggesting those initial hurdles didn't turn around and instead hinted at persistent difficulties in keeping up with the demands of legal training. Your middle family income added another layer, as it didn't provide the extra safety net that sometimes helps students push through tough spots in ways our model has seen before. Then there's your LSAT score of 36, which, while respectable, pulled in the same concerning direction when combined with everything else, like a puzzle piece that fit the tougher outlook. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 echoed this pattern, showing a consistent thread of academic effort that hasn't yet broken through to the levels typically needed for bar success. Even factors like being a white male pursuing full-time studies wove in subtly, aligning with profiles our model associates with higher risks in this journey. With all these elements together—your deciles leading the way—the model landed at a 46.5% chance of not passing the bar, which is why we had to make this call to protect spots for those with clearer shots. It's not about your worth or potential; it's just the story these details told. The good news? You've got time to shift this narrative—maybe retake the LSAT, build stronger grades, or seek targeted prep that addresses those early decile flags. You're capable of rewriting this story, Alex, and I believe in that for you. Hang in there.
